# Why Classical Architecture Loops Strain {#sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"Feedback is the control of a system by reinserting into the system the results of its performance. If these results are merely used as numerical data for the criticism of the system and its regulation, we have the simple feedback of the control engineers."*
>
> — Norbert Wiener, *The Human Use of Human Beings* (1950)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** Norbert Wiener, who founded cybernetics, made feedback the defining property of control. This chapter asks where a modern architecture loop loses that property when its state, choices, measurements, reviews, or decisions no longer keep pace.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{Architecture 2.0 is motivated by pressure inside computer architecture, not by enthusiasm for AI. The classical architecture loop remains valuable, but it strains as specialization, heterogeneous integration, memory movement, physical constraints, software velocity, and verification burden converge. Before adding AI, an architect must determine which part of that loop cannot keep up: representation, action, feedback, rejection, or commitment. Generative methods can intensify the pressure when they increase proposal volume without relieving the bound that already limits credible progress.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
Before adding AI, which part of the existing design loop cannot keep up:
representation, action, feedback, rejection, or commitment?
:::

Every architecture project already runs some form of design loop. The team
proposes a candidate, evaluates it with workloads and tools, rejects or revises
it in light of the results, and commits only to what the evidence supports.
@sec-moonshot introduced this loop through a compact lighthouse prompt. It asks
for a low-power, RISC-V-based compute subsystem for real-time mobile XR within a
3\ W, 3\ nm-class mobile envelope, presented as a design-space report with
evidence and rejected alternatives. The request is brief, but answering it
requires a great deal of architectural state.

A larger model may produce a longer answer, but architects must still
coordinate the workloads, tools, evidence, and review behind it. They already
use models, simulators, benchmarks, profilers, spreadsheets, compilers,
register-transfer level (RTL) flows, electronic design automation (EDA) tools,
and expert review. As projects become more complex, teams must track more design
information, consider more alternatives, run more expensive evaluations,
discard more weak candidates, and justify more consequential decisions. At
some point, the existing process may no longer keep up.

The familiar loop remains useful. When AI takes on part of the work, the team
must preserve the inputs, tool outputs, rejected candidates, and decisions that
another architect would need to inspect the result.

> **Scissors gap.** The scissors gap is the widening gap between the rate at
> which design choices, constraints, and evidence demands expand and the rate at
> which a team can obtain trusted feedback, reject weak candidates, and commit
> to a decision supported by the evidence.

The gap can open at several points. A loop may omit state that later proves
decisive, offer the wrong choices, wait too long for measurements, accumulate
unevaluated candidates, or leave the next decision unclear. A flood of
proposals is especially troublesome when the team is already struggling to
reject weak candidates, but not every strained loop has that cause.

The useful question is which part of the loop cannot keep up, not where a model
could be inserted. A loop may be representation-bound, action-bound,
feedback-bound, rejection-bound, or commitment-bound. Each diagnosis has a
different symptom, requires different evidence, and suggests a different first
response (@tbl-loop-bottleneck-diagnostic).

| **Bound** | **Symptom** | **Lighthouse example** | **Record to inspect** | **First fix** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Representation-bound | The loop cannot encode the state that decides the outcome, so later evidence reverses its ranking. | It ranks XR compute organizations without representing memory-movement cost, so a later estimate overturns the proxy order. | Inputs, assumptions, workload state, interfaces, and omitted variables. | Add or refresh the state needed to explain the reversal. |
| Action-bound | The available choices are too narrow, too broad, or insufficiently constrained to reach a valid candidate. | The loop can retune parameters but cannot change the accelerator interface, so it never reaches the organization that would meet both the real-time deadline and the power envelope. | Choices the loop can actually make, invalid moves, and fixed interfaces. | Revise the allowed choices or the boundary of the task. |
| Feedback-bound | Relevant measurements are too scarce, slow, or expensive to keep up with the decisions that need them. | Only a few simulation-stage estimates are affordable, so most candidates remain weakly judged. | Measurement queues, latency, cost, coverage, and known proxy errors. | Reduce, redirect, or increase measurement capacity where it changes decisions. |
| Rejection-bound | Comparable proposals arrive faster than trusted review can settle them, so the queue grows and weak candidates spill into expensive stages. | Lacking an early check, an accelerator that leads a cheap proxy ranking is not rejected until an expensive run reveals its data-movement cost. | Arrival rate, review rate, queue growth, and reasons for acceptance or rejection. | Control admission or increase trusted review capacity. |
| Commitment-bound | The process cannot say what the current evidence permits, so it over- or under-commits. | A candidate clears available checks, but nothing states whether that authorizes more exploration or an implementation decision. | Decision records, cited evidence, rollback cost, and a named owner. | Limit the decision to what the current evidence supports. |

: **A diagnostic for which part of the loop cannot keep up:** Each row connects a symptom to its lighthouse example, the records needed to confirm it, and a practical first response. {#tbl-loop-bottleneck-diagnostic tbl-colwidths="[15,25,26,16,18]"}

These bounds are diagnoses rather than five independent stages. A single
problem can affect several at once, and fixing one may move the bottleneck
elsewhere. The first task is to locate the part that currently limits progress.
@sec-architecture-20-ontology uses that diagnosis to define the minimum
information needed to review an architectural claim.

The rest of the chapter traces these pressures to their sources. Product
schedules limit the time available for decisions. Specialization and chiplets
increase the number of alternatives. Software changes can invalidate workload
assumptions, while physical evaluation consumes scarce engineering time. Teams
must therefore design both the artifact and the process used to evaluate it.
Adding AI before locating the bound can create more work without improving the
study.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can use the scissors gap to locate pressure in an
architecture loop before deciding where AI belongs. That means you can:

- **Recognize** the scissors gap between choices and trusted feedback in a real architecture setting;
- **Diagnose** whether a loop is limited by representation, action, feedback, rejection, or commitment;
- **Connect** release timing, search scale, workload drift, physical constraints, and engineering cost to the bound they stress;
- **Identify** where generic AI assumptions break at architecture boundaries;
- **Decide** whether AI would relieve the diagnosed bound or shift pressure elsewhere.
:::

## Classical Loops Already Use Feedback

Computer architecture has always advanced through feedback and revision. A
typical study begins with an aim such as improving latency, reducing energy,
raising throughput, supporting a workload, or fitting a system within a power
and cost envelope. The architect chooses an abstraction, builds or selects a
model, runs an analysis or simulation, studies the result, and revises the
design. Work that survives this cycle eventually reaches implementation,
validation, verification, and signoff. Textbooks and industrial practice both
follow this basic pattern
[@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach].

Consider a traditional SPEC CPU study. An architect selects workloads, proposes
a cache-hierarchy or branch-predictor change, runs a simulator or performance
model, and inspects instructions per cycle (IPC), miss rates,
branch-misprediction rates, and area and power estimates. Candidates that help
one workload while harming others may be revised or discarded. Human judgment
shapes the study throughout, from workload selection and proxy choice to the
decision that a candidate deserves deeper analysis. SPEC CPU 2017 provides an
industry-standard suite for compute-intensive performance across processor,
memory-system, and compiler behavior [@SPEC2017CPU]. The important feature here
is the review standard. It combines a bounded workload set, a stated model,
comparable metrics, explicit rejection of weak candidates, and expert judgment
about whether the evidence is sufficient.

Architecture projects already record some design intent, models, candidates,
tool runs, and review decisions. Broader use of AI makes several other details
important, including workload and interface assumptions, tool versions and
costs, rejected alternatives, the scope of each decision, and the person
responsible for it. These records support established review by showing where
missing state, scarce feedback, unresolved alternatives, or unclear authority
is causing trouble. One of the five bounds begins to limit progress when the
state, choices, evaluation cost, or evidence burden exceeds the capacity of the
team and its tools.

## Release Intervals Help Isolate Risk

Product schedules show how teams limit the risk attached to each decision.
Intel's tick-tock model is a familiar example. A "tick" moved a known
microarchitecture to a new process technology, while a "tock" introduced a new
microarchitecture on a more mature process. Separating the two reduced the
number of major changes attempted at once and allowed one release to inform the
next. When node transitions lengthened, Intel shifted toward
process-architecture-optimization, keeping 14\ nm and 10\ nm technologies in
use longer while continuing to optimize products and processes
[@Intel2015Form10K].

The schedule did more than set delivery dates. It limited what could change,
what evidence permitted the next step, and how
the organization responded when feedback became slower or more expensive. A
change in release schedule can therefore reveal that the previous allocation of
technical risk no longer fits the work.

Earlier architectural shifts also changed how work was organized.
@tbl-historical-loop-transformations shows how each one established an
interface, rule, workload, tool path, or decision criterion. In each case, a
constraint that had been handled informally became inspectable through a shared
technical artifact or rule.

| **Shift** | **What the loop defined** | **Architecture 2.0 lesson** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| System/360 compatibility | A stable Instruction Set Architecture (ISA) contract separated architecture from implementation across a product family [@AmdahlBlaauwBrooks1964System360]. | The ISA carries architectural commitments across implementations and product generations. |
| Mead--Conway VLSI and MOSIS | Design rules, layout abstractions, and fabrication access turned custom-chip design into a shareable and reusable loop [@MeadConway1980VLSI; @USCISI2025MOSISFirstYear]. | Shared representations and fabrication access broaden participation in architecture work. |
| RISC | Workload, compiler, Very Large-Scale Integration (VLSI), and the quantitative cost/performance case became the architectural argument [@PattersonDitzel1980RISC]. | A complete comparison includes the workload, compiler, and implementation cost. |
| SPEC-style benchmarking | Workload selection, run rules, reporting conventions, and comparability became community infrastructure [@SPEC2017CPU]. | Benchmarks govern workload choice, execution, and comparison. |
| Logic synthesis and timing closure | Hardware Description Language (HDL), libraries, constraints, and timing reports gave downstream tools authority to reject upstream choices [@DeMicheli1994SynthesisOptimization]. | Architecture candidates should receive implementation feedback before commitment. |
| CUDA-style GPU programming | Kernels, thread hierarchies, memory spaces, libraries, and toolchains made specialized hardware programmable [@NickollsEtAl2008CUDA]. | Specialized hardware needs a usable programming model and toolchain. |

: **Each shift made part of the architecture workflow explicit.** These changes gave the community a shared representation, interface, tool path, benchmark, or decision criterion. {#tbl-historical-loop-transformations tbl-colwidths="[22,39,28]"}

Architecture 2.0 continues this pattern. AI methods can propose, search,
summarize, and optimize at a scale that exposes whichever part of the existing
loop is already closest to its limit.

## Architecture Levers Add State

Representation becomes the limiting factor when a study lacks the state needed
to distinguish promising candidates from misleading ones. For decades,
technology scaling improved established design styles with smaller, faster,
cheaper, and more energy-efficient transistors. As Dennard scaling weakened
(@sec-moonshot), the field leaned harder on microarchitecture, instruction-level
parallelism, caches, speculation, vector units, multicore, accelerators,
specialization, and system-level optimization. Each option adds design choices,
assumptions, and interactions that the study must represent.

Each additional choice introduces interactions that must be analyzed. More
elaborate microarchitecture adds policies and corner cases. Multicore adds
coherence, synchronization, memory ordering, and workload partitioning.
Specialization improves efficiency when the workload and software stack are
understood, but it adds interfaces, data movement, programmability, and
verification burden. Chiplets and heterogeneous integration promise
modularity and scaling beyond a monolithic die, but they add partitioning, die-to-die
interfaces, package-level constraints, test, yield, thermal coupling, and
supply-chain questions.

These levers remain attractive because efficiency still matters
(@fig-architecture-levers). The same choices that recover efficiency also make
the design state harder to represent and the results harder to evaluate.

![**Each architecture lever adds information to the study.** Scaling, microarchitecture, optimization, specialization, and composition create new opportunities for efficiency. Each also adds constraints, interfaces, tool feedback, rejected alternatives, and evidence that a credible comparison must include.](images/F2-architecture-levers){#fig-architecture-levers width="100%" fig-alt="Horizontal chain of architecture levers from scaling through composition, showing that each efficiency lever adds design state and evidence requirements."}

The post-Dennard turn toward specialization (@sec-moonshot) explains why recent
levers cluster near the specialization and composition end of the chain. This
is the period Hennessy and Patterson described as a new golden age for computer
architecture [@HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge]. It follows the same
quantitative tradition as the RISC revolution. RISC paired a simplified
instruction set with the compilers, benchmarks, and simulators needed to test
its architectural claims. Architecture 1.0 established tools for measuring IPC
and execution time, grounding comparisons in the Iron Law of Performance
[@EmerClark1984VAXCharacterization] ($\text{Time} = \frac{\text{Instructions}}{\text{Program}} \times \frac{\text{Cycles}}{\text{Instruction}} \times \frac{\text{Time}}{\text{Cycle}}$).

Architecture 2.0 extends that quantitative discipline to the process used to
compare candidates. Today's wider set of architectural levers adds workload,
interface, and implementation details that a credible comparison may need. If a
study omits those details, the architect must add them before expanding the
search. Otherwise, AI explores an incompletely described problem.

## Specialization and Chiplets Expand Search

The available choices become a constraint when a study cannot express or limit
them well enough to reach a valid design. Specialization makes this problem
easy to see. A battery- and thermally limited mobile part and a warehouse-scale
system bounded by power delivery and total cost of ownership (TCO) demand
different measures of efficiency
[@BarrosoHolzleRanganathan2019DatacenterAsComputer]. Specialization therefore
changes both the metrics that matter and the number of decisions a study must
evaluate.

An architect must decide what to specialize, where the specialized function
belongs, and how it communicates with the rest of the system. Choosing between
a CPU and an accelerator is only the start of designing a low-power XR
subsystem. The decision also raises questions about vector length,
memory hierarchy, local buffers, compression, dataflow, quantization, runtime
scheduling, compiler support, sensor streams, display deadlines, thermal
behavior, and fallback modes.

Chiplets compound the effect. They make it possible to compose systems from
multiple dies and to mix process technologies, IP blocks, and memory
technologies. But a chiplet system is not simply a bigger board-level system
inside a package. The package changes latency, bandwidth, energy, thermal
coupling, test, repair, physical constraints, and business boundaries. Open
standards such as Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) aim to make die-to-die integration more composable by
standardizing interface layers, protocols, software models, and compliance
expectations [@UCIeConsortium2026Spec]. The architect must still decide what to
partition, which interface to use, and what evidence will show that the
partition works.

Even a narrow study can contain many combinations. The following arithmetic is
illustrative rather than measured. Suppose a team explores an accelerator and
memory subsystem for one XRBench workload family
[@KwonEtAl2023XRBench]. If it considers five compute
organizations, four vector or accelerator interface choices, six memory
hierarchy choices, four interconnect choices, three voltage/frequency
policies, three compiler/runtime policies, and three verification or fidelity
levels, the product is already:

```{python}
#| echo: false
toy_design_loop_states = 5 * 4 * 6 * 4 * 3 * 3 * 3
```

$$
5 \times 4 \times 6 \times 4 \times 3 \times 3 \times 3
$$
That is `{python} f"{toy_design_loop_states:,}"` candidate configurations before
counting workload versions, process corners, thermal constraints, or reliability
cases, and before rejecting a single invalid configuration. Real studies can
grow far larger or take far longer even before silicon measurements are
available.

The count alone says little about how much useful work the team can complete. If
each surviving configuration needs an analytical model, simulator run,
synthesis check, or human review, a larger choice set also increases the demand
for feedback. Cheap models reduce evaluation cost, but they do not establish
whether a choice is legal or whether stronger measurements will preserve its
ranking. The sequence from
@sec-data-representations-world-models through
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization develops those representation,
environment, and method questions after this chapter has located the pressure.

For the lighthouse study, these combinations correspond to consequential
design choices. Choosing a CPU, accelerator, or System-on-Chip (SoC)
organization fixes interfaces, memory paths, compiler and runtime assumptions,
physical constraints, and which candidates the available tools can evaluate.

Other architecture tasks create similar pressure in different ways
(@tbl-design-loop-scale-anchors). Mapping, design-space exploration (DSE),
physical design, and software tuning do not share one measurement scale, but
each combines many possible choices with constraints that make only some of
them useful. Timeloop, an analytical framework for mapping and evaluating
deep-neural-network (DNN) accelerators, provides a concrete example. Consider a
single convolutional-neural-network (CNN) layer with seven nested loop
dimensions and a four-level memory hierarchy. At each memory level, the mapper
can permute the seven loop dimensions. It must also choose which levels each of
the three tensors, inputs, weights, and outputs, will bypass. Before tiling
sizes are counted, the unconstrained mapspace already contains $(7!)^4 \times
(2^4)^3$ arrangements [@ParasharEtAl2019Timeloop].

| **Loop example** | **Scale anchor** | **Loop lesson** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| DNN accelerator mapping | Timeloop enumerates loop permutations, factorization choices, and level-bypass alternatives. | Mapping is itself a combinatorial problem; architecture evaluation depends on the mapper and its constraints. |
| DNN accelerator DSE | MAESTRO, an analytical cost model for estimating DNN dataflow performance, reports 480M candidates, 2.5M valid designs, and 0.17M designs/s [@KwonEtAl2019MAESTRO]. | Pruning rules determine which candidates are evaluated and which are never seen. |
| TPU-block floorplanning | Learned floorplanning reduced macro-placement production from months to hours [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement]. | Routed physical quality and strong baselines still require separate evaluation. |
| Tensor-program tuning | AutoTVM, a machine learning-based tensor program optimizer, describes tensor-operator search spaces on the order of billions of possible implementations for a single GPU operator [@ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM]. | Compiler schedules create a hardware-dependent search problem of their own. |

: **Large search spaces place different demands on evaluation:** Mapping, accelerator DSE, floorplanning, and tensor-program tuning combine candidate scale, validity constraints, feedback cost, and different evidence requirements. {#tbl-design-loop-scale-anchors tbl-colwidths="[25,36,28]"}

These examples differ in task, fidelity, and tool chain, yet all combine a
large search space with invalid candidates and costly evaluation. MAESTRO also
shows that rapid search need not solve the harder problem. At 0.17M designs per
second, its full 480M-candidate space can be examined in under an hour. Cheap
analytical feedback is plentiful in that case. The difficult decision is which
apparent winners justify more expensive evaluation. On the common logarithmic
axis in @fig-search-vs-eval-gap, only the deliberately conservative toy loop
approaches the illustrative budget for trusted evaluation.

```{python}
#| label: fig-search-vs-eval-gap
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Search spaces outgrow affordable trusted evaluation.** The scale anchors appear on one log axis, from the toy SoC subsystem count computed above to Timeloop's unconstrained mapspace [@ParasharEtAl2019Timeloop; @KwonEtAl2019MAESTRO; @ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM], against a shaded band marking how many trusted reviews one study can afford. Filled markers are stated or computed counts; the hollow marker is AutoTVM's order-of-magnitude estimate. The band is an illustrative assumption, not a measurement.
#| out-width: "92%"
#| fig-alt: "Log-scale strip chart plotting four search-space sizes as points: a toy SoC loop at about 13 thousand configurations, MAESTRO's 480 million candidates, AutoTVM's roughly one billion tensor programs, and Timeloop's 2.6 times ten to the eighteenth unconstrained mappings. A shaded green band from one hundred to one hundred thousand marks an illustrative affordable trusted-evaluation budget; only the toy loop falls inside it, and the Timeloop mapspace lies about thirteen orders of magnitude beyond it."

from math import factorial

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style, row_axis, top_log_axis

# Data provenance (all values transcribed or computed from this chapter's text):
# - Toy SoC subsystem loop: 5*4*6*4*3*3*3 = 12,960, computed earlier in this chapter.
# - MAESTRO accelerator DSE: 480M candidates, stated [@KwonEtAl2019MAESTRO].
# - AutoTVM tensor programs: "on the order of billions" per GPU operator, stated
#   qualitatively (order-of-magnitude, hollow marker) [@ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM].
# - Timeloop unconstrained mapspace: (7!)^4 * (2^4)^3 ~= 2.6e18, computed from the
#   stated expression [@ParasharEtAl2019Timeloop]. Values not recomputed from sources.
# - Trusted-evaluation band 1e2..1e5 reviewed cases per study: illustrative assumption
#   (minutes to hours per reviewed case over a weeks-long study budget), not a
#   measurement. As-of: 2026-07-14.

toy_states = 5 * 4 * 6 * 4 * 3 * 3 * 3
timeloop_mapspace = factorial(7) ** 4 * (2**4) ** 3

rows = [
    {"label": "Toy SoC subsystem loop", "note": "computed in this chapter", "value": toy_states, "right": "$1.3\\times10^{4}$", "stated": True},
    {"label": "MAESTRO accelerator DSE", "note": "480M candidates, stated", "value": 4.8e8, "right": "$4.8\\times10^{8}$", "stated": True},
    {"label": "AutoTVM tensor programs", "note": "order of billions, approximate", "value": 1e9, "right": "$\\sim\\!10^{9}$", "stated": False},
    {"label": "Timeloop unconstrained mapspace", "note": "$(7!)^{4}\\times(2^{4})^{3}$, computed", "value": timeloop_mapspace, "right": "$2.6\\times10^{18}$", "stated": True},
]

band_low, band_high = 1e2, 1e5

apply_style()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.95, 2.75))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.42, right=0.82, top=0.79, bottom=0.22)

row_axis(ax, len(rows))
top_log_axis(
    ax,
    xlim=(2e1, 4e19),
    xticks=[1e2, 1e5, 1e8, 1e11, 1e14, 1e17],
    xticklabels=["$10^{2}$", "$10^{5}$", "$10^{8}$", "$10^{11}$", "$10^{14}$", "$10^{17}$"],
    xlabel="search-space size (candidate configurations)",
)

# Affordable trusted-evaluation band: canonical evidence tint (#E7F5EC from the
# shared tint set; no COLORS key exists for tints) paired with the evidence ink edge.
ax.axvspan(band_low, band_high, facecolor="#E7F5EC", zorder=0)
for edge in (band_low, band_high):
    ax.axvline(edge, color=COLORS["evidence_ink"], linewidth=0.8, linestyle=(0, (4, 3)), zorder=1)
ax.text(
    (band_low * band_high) ** 0.5,
    3.42,
    "affordable trusted-evaluation\nband (illustrative)",
    ha="center",
    va="center",
    fontsize=5.8,
    fontweight="bold",
    color=COLORS["evidence_ink"],
    linespacing=1.2,
    bbox={"facecolor": "white", "edgecolor": "none", "pad": 0.8, "alpha": 0.85},
    zorder=4,
)

for y, row in enumerate(rows):
    ax.axhline(y, color=COLORS["row"], linewidth=0.65, zorder=0)
    if row["stated"]:
        ax.scatter([row["value"]], [y], s=26, marker="o", facecolor=COLORS["designspace"], edgecolor=COLORS["designspace"], linewidth=1.0, zorder=3)
    else:
        ax.scatter([row["value"]], [y], s=26, marker="o", facecolors="white", edgecolors=COLORS["designspace"], linewidth=1.2, zorder=3)
    ax.text(-0.86, y - 0.17, row["label"], transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform(), ha="left", va="center", fontsize=6.6, fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["ink"], clip_on=False)
    ax.text(-0.86, y + 0.23, row["note"], transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform(), ha="left", va="center", fontsize=5.4, color=COLORS["muted"], clip_on=False)
    ax.text(1.04, y, row["right"], transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform(), ha="left", va="center", fontsize=6.1, fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["designspace_ink"], clip_on=False)

# Gap annotation on the widest anchor row: band edge to the Timeloop mapspace.
ax.annotate(
    "",
    xy=(timeloop_mapspace / 6, 3.0),
    xytext=(band_high, 3.0),
    arrowprops={"arrowstyle": "<->", "color": COLORS["muted"], "linewidth": 0.8},
)
ax.text(
    (band_high * timeloop_mapspace) ** 0.5,
    2.68,
    "$\\approx$13 orders of magnitude beyond the band",
    ha="center",
    va="center",
    fontsize=5.8,
    color=COLORS["muted"],
)

add_note_box(
    fig,
    "Band assumes $10^{2}$ to $10^{5}$ trusted reviews per study (minutes to hours each over a weeks-long budget); an assumption, not a measured limit.",
    xywh=(0.12, 0.040, 0.76, 0.085),
    fontsize=5.4,
)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

The other search spaces exceed the illustrative evaluation budget by roughly
four to thirteen orders of magnitude. The architect must then determine whether
the study admits too many unhelpful choices or cannot afford enough reliable
evaluations to distinguish among them.

## Specialized Hardware Needs a Software Loop

The available choices extend beyond hardware. An accelerator, vector unit,
memory hierarchy, or chiplet partition may look efficient in isolation but
still be difficult to use. Programmers, compilers, runtimes, libraries, and
deployment systems must reach the mechanism without losing its benefit to data
movement, synchronization, code-generation overhead, or maintenance cost.

The historical examples in @tbl-historical-loop-transformations show the same
dependence. RISC needed effective compiler support. CUDA, NVIDIA's parallel
computing platform and programming model, made GPU specialization accessible
through a programming model and toolchain [@NickollsEtAl2008CUDA]. Modern
tensor programs add a large software search problem of their own. Halide, a
language and compiler for image-processing pipelines, separates algorithms
from their schedules. MLIR provides reusable compiler infrastructure for
representing and lowering operations across levels of abstraction. Both make
scheduling, lowering, and intermediate representations part of the performance
study [@RaganKelleyEtAl2017HalideCACM; @LattnerEtAl2020MLIR].

::: {.callout-field-note title="The Compiler Assumption Behind Itanium"}
Itanium's EPIC (Explicitly Parallel Instruction Computing) architecture
depended on compilers exposing enough instruction-level parallelism to keep a
very wide machine busy [@SchlanskerRau2000EPIC]. Its performance could not be
studied through issue width alone. The relevant evidence included the
parallelism that compilers found in representative workloads.

Intel's Larrabee similarly assumed that software rasterization on many x86
cores would match fixed-function GPUs [@SeilerEtAl2008Larrabee]. Transmeta's
Crusoe assumed that code-morphing
software would let a simple core match x86 [@DehnertEtAl2003Transmeta]. In all
three designs, software capability determined which hardware choices could be
evaluated credibly.

A hardware proposal is action-bound when the software path needed to use it
lies outside the choices the study can inspect and evaluate.
:::

For the lighthouse prompt, a "64-bit RISC-V compute subsystem" cannot be judged
by hardware structure alone. If the answer proposes a vector
extension, custom accelerator, memory-local dataflow, or chiplet boundary, the
loop must also represent how code reaches that mechanism, what compiler or
runtime assumptions are required, which libraries or kernels use it, and which
tests reject a design that is efficient only in a hand-written kernel. The
software path is part of the architectural claim.

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="RISC-V is a software contract"}
**Context.** The ISA phrase specifies more than an instruction encoding. It
marks the boundary where hardware choices affect compilers, runtimes, libraries,
operating systems, and compatibility tests.

**In the lighthouse prompt.** "64-bit RISC-V-based" and "vector-capable CPU,
accelerator, or SoC block" make the software path part of the claim. The study
must explain how code reaches the mechanism, which application binary interface
(ABI), memory-model, and toolchain assumptions it preserves, and which tests
would catch unsupported semantics.

The ISA is therefore part of the evidence that the proposed subsystem can
actually be used.
:::

## Software Changes Faster Than Silicon {#sec-software-changes-faster-than-silicon}

A hardware study can become outdated while the chip is still being designed.
AI models, precision formats, sparsity patterns, compiler passes, kernel
libraries, runtimes, serving systems, batching strategies, fleet policies, and
benchmarks all change faster than a typical silicon program. Precision formats
such as FP8, INT4, and block-scale formats[^fn-hw-block-scale-formats] may change
even though the hardware must commit to its supported data types years in
advance. Sparsity patterns, the structured or unstructured zeros in weights or
activations that hardware can skip, can change as models and software evolve.
When a long-lived hardware decision still rests on an outdated workload, missing
state has become a commitment risk.

[^fn-hw-block-scale-formats]: Block-scale formats share an exponent across a block of low-precision mantissas, preserving dynamic range with a small memory footprint. Supporting one in silicon fixes datapath choices years before deployment, so the study must consider whether the format and workload are likely to remain relevant.

Generative AI may widen this timing mismatch. Lowering the cost of producing
code can lead to more software and faster churn, a Jevons-like
effect[^fn-econ-jevons-paradox], rather than leaving the workload unchanged at a
lower cost. The 2024 Stack Overflow Developer Survey reported that most
professional developers already used AI tools in their workflow
[@StackOverflow2024DeveloperSurvey]. A silicon program can therefore target a
software snapshot that has changed by deployment. Faster hardware design alone
cannot remove this risk because fabrication and physical closure set a lower
bound on the hardware schedule.

[^fn-econ-jevons-paradox]: In *The Coal Question* (1865), economist W. S. Jevons observed that making a resource cheaper to use can increase its total consumption. Applied here, cheaper code generation may increase software volume and churn, which shortens the useful life of the workload assumptions behind a specialized design.

This is a familiar architectural tradeoff. Specialization can improve
efficiency while making a design less tolerant of workload drift. Generality
preserves flexibility, often at a cost in performance per watt. A study should
therefore bound the workload, measure how it changes, and revisit the hardware
decision when its original assumptions no longer hold.

Software can also adapt to deployed hardware. Compilers, kernels, and runtimes
often improve after launch and recover performance that the initial evaluation
did not capture. A study should distinguish improvements made for the hardware
from unrelated changes in the workload. The former can strengthen the case for
an existing design, while the latter can make its original evidence stale.
@sec-loop-patterns-across-stack returns to this distinction in the
deployment-facing loops.

The scale of the target workload changes as well. The rapid growth of AI
training compute shows how quickly one workload assumption can become obsolete.
Selected estimates in Epoch AI's database span more than twenty orders of
magnitude between the Mark I Perceptron and a 2025 frontier model
(@fig-ai-compute-growth). An analysis of the broader database estimated annual
growth of roughly four- to fivefold for recent notable and frontier models
through May 2024, with results that depend on model class, outliers, and the
form of the fit [@SevillaRoldan2024ComputeGrowth]. Growth in compute is not a
direct measure of workload drift. It does show why evidence tied to one model
scale, software stack, or training regime cannot be applied to another without
checking the assumptions. For the lighthouse study, that means recording the
model, compiler, runtime, benchmark, trace, and deployment versions used for
each decision.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ai-compute-growth
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Selected training-compute estimates span more than twenty orders of magnitude:** The points are historical and modern anchors from Epoch AI's daily-updated AI Models Dataset, frozen for this book on July 14, 2026 [@EpochAI2025AIModels]. Filled markers are records whose database confidence field is `Confident`; hollow markers are `Likely` or `Speculative`. The selection is not an exhaustive sample or a fitted trend. Epoch's broader analysis, rather than this hand-selected series, supports the separate estimate of roughly four- to fivefold annual growth for recent notable and frontier models through May 2024 [@SevillaRoldan2024ComputeGrowth].
#| out-width: "78%"
#| fig-alt: "Log-scale scatter of selected AI training-compute estimates, spanning more than twenty orders of magnitude from the 1957 Mark I Perceptron to GPT-4.5 in 2025. Filled markers denote records labeled Confident by Epoch AI; hollow markers denote Likely or Speculative records."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style

apply_style()

# Source receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter2-epoch-ai-model-anchors.csv
# (year, training-compute FLOP, label, show_label, database confidence).
# Values are selected records from Epoch AI's AI Models Dataset snapshot
# retrieved 2026-07-14. The selection illustrates scale; it is not used to fit
# or estimate a population trend.
points = [
    (1957, 6.948949e5, "Perceptron Mark I", True, "Likely"),
    (2012, 4.7e17, "AlexNet", True, "Confident"),
    (2014, 1.2291e19, "VGG16", False, "Confident"),
    (2016, 1.9e21, "AlphaGo Lee", False, "Speculative"),
    (2017, 7.4245248e18, "Transformer", False, "Confident"),
    (2018, 2.85e20, "BERT-Large", True, "Confident"),
    (2019, 1.92e21, "GPT-2 1.5B", False, "Speculative"),
    (2020, 3.14e23, "GPT-3 175B", True, "Confident"),
    (2021, 6.31e23, "Gopher 280B", False, "Confident"),
    (2022, 2.5272e24, "PaLM 540B", False, "Confident"),
    (2023, 2.1e25, "GPT-4", True, "Likely"),
    (2024, 3.8e25, "Llama 3.1 405B", False, "Confident"),
    (2025, 3.8e26, "GPT-4.5", True, "Likely"),
]

confident = [p for p in points if p[4] == "Confident"]
qualified = [p for p in points if p[4] != "Confident"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.65, 2.45))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.16, right=0.96, top=0.95, bottom=0.18)
ax.scatter([p[0] for p in confident], [p[1] for p in confident],
           s=24, color=COLORS["blue"], zorder=3)
ax.scatter([p[0] for p in qualified], [p[1] for p in qualified],
           s=24, facecolors="white",
           edgecolors=COLORS["blue"], linewidths=1.1, zorder=3)
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_ylim(1e4, 1e28)
ax.set_xlim(1953, 2028)
ax.set_ylabel("training compute (FLOP)", fontsize=7)
ax.set_xticks([1960, 1980, 2000, 2020])
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6.5, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
# (dx pts, dy pts, ha): late-2010s labels sit left of their points so they
# stay inside the axes and clear of neighboring markers.
offsets = {
    "Perceptron Mark I": (8, -3, "left"),
    "AlexNet": (7, -8, "left"),
    "BERT-Large": (7, -3, "left"),
    "GPT-3 175B": (7, -2, "left"),
    "GPT-4": (-6, -3, "right"),
    "GPT-4.5": (-6, 4, "right"),
}
for x, y, lab, show, confidence in points:
    if show:
        dx, dy, ha = offsets.get(lab, (5, -2, "left"))
        ax.annotate(lab, (x, y), textcoords="offset points", xytext=(dx, dy), fontsize=5.7, color=COLORS["ink"], ha=ha)

ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.6)
for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
```

Compiler 2.0 offers a related warning about changing abstractions. Amarasinghe
argues that compilers once hid much of the hardware from programmers, whereas
multicore processors, vector instructions, accelerators, and heterogeneous
systems have returned more of the performance burden to software developers
[@Amarasinghe2020Compiler20; @Amarasinghe2026Compiler20CSAIL]. Architects face
a similar shift. Abstractions remain essential, but studies must now represent
more of the workload, software, hardware, and physical state that earlier
abstractions could safely hide.

Benchmarks provide one way to keep changing workloads comparable. MLPerf,
introduced in @sec-moonshot, defines shared tasks and reporting rules for
machine-learning systems [@MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf]. Its inference benchmark
addressed a field with more than 100 organizations building ML inference chips
and systems spanning at least three orders of magnitude in power and five in
performance. The first submission round produced more than 600 reproducible
measurements from 14 organizations [@ReddiEtAl2020MLPerfInference]. Those
measurements are comparable because the benchmark specifies scenarios, latency
constraints, accuracy targets, software stacks, and reporting rules
[@ReddiEtAl2021VisionBehindMLPerf]. A benchmark remains useful only while the
community maintains the tasks and run rules that make results comparable.[^fn-inst-nist]
When those rules change, an earlier comparison may no longer answer the same
question.

[^fn-inst-nist]: Metrology offers a useful analogy. The U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) maintains reference standards and calibration chains so that a stated unit has the same meaning across laboratories. Architecture benchmarks require similar maintenance if their results are to remain comparable over time.

For the lighthouse prompt, "XR" is too broad to define a workload. The workload
is a moving bundle of sensing, perception, graphics, display, interaction, latency,
quality-of-experience, and energy constraints. Even with XRBench
[@KwonEtAl2023XRBench], a credible architecture loop must still decide
which traces, model versions, deadlines, input distributions, and quality
targets matter. If the software stack changes faster than the hardware loop
can absorb, the design may optimize yesterday's workload.

## Physical Constraints Move Into Architecture {#sec-physical-constraints-move-into-architecture}

Physical effects can strain a study in two ways. An early model may omit an
effect that later overturns the ranking, leaving the design poorly represented.
Or the measurement needed to resolve the issue may arrive too late or cost too
much, making feedback the limiting factor. Architecture connects software
intent and hardware mechanisms to these physical constraints. It does not
replace the specialist EDA and implementation flows needed to verify them.

Data-movement estimates, for example, can reverse an early ranking based on
compute alone. Moving data through a memory hierarchy often costs far more
energy than arithmetic. Horowitz's widely used estimates quantify the
difference [@Horowitz2014Energy]. A study must therefore ask not
only which compute block is fastest, but also where the data resides, how often
it moves, who schedules it, what locality is available, which precision is
acceptable, and what the software stack can express.

A useful architecture-level decomposition is
$$
E_{\mathrm{system}} =
E_{\mathrm{compute}} + E_{\mathrm{memory}} +
E_{\mathrm{interconnect}} + E_{\mathrm{control}} +
E_{\mathrm{leakage}} .
$$
This decomposition is a diagnostic rather than a circuit-level energy model. It
helps an architect check whether the comparison includes every major source of
energy that could change the decision. A candidate that reduces arithmetic but
increases memory movement, interconnect traffic, control overhead, or leakage
may make the system worse. Control engineers use the term *waterbed effect* for
a related frequency-domain pattern [@Bode1945NetworkAnalysis]. The analogy here
is qualitative. No conservation law requires memory energy to rise when compute
energy falls. An optimization focused on one term may nevertheless allow the
others to grow enough to exceed the baseline total
(@fig-waterbed-effect).

![**The waterbed effect in the energy decomposition:** The illustrative candidate reduces compute energy but increases memory and interconnect energy enough to exceed the baseline total. A study that omits data movement can therefore rank the worse of the two designs first. Bar heights are illustrative rather than measured energy breakdowns.](images/F2-waterbed-effect){#fig-waterbed-effect width="100%" fig-alt="Bar chart comparing a baseline energy breakdown with an AI-optimized candidate whose lower compute energy is outweighed by higher memory and interconnect energy."}

Interconnect costs deserve the same attention as memory movement. On-chip
networks, package links, memory interfaces, collectives, and host-device
protocols limit what a design can sustain. Timing, placement, routing, IR drop,
thermal behavior, power delivery, leakage, signoff, and test may also overturn
an architectural choice, so architects must consider the relevant EDA and
physical-design constraints early. Power and thermal density can flag a dense
accelerator mapping as risky before implementation. Later IR-drop and thermal
signoff can confirm or clear that risk once a placed netlist and current profile
exist. Because physical-design tools rely on heuristic optimization, one failed
closure attempt cannot reveal whether the candidate or the tool setup caused
the failure. Comparing constraints, seeds, and repeated outcomes helps separate
the two.

These cases point to different problems. Missing physical assumptions indicate
a representation bound. A long wait for the measurement that settles the
question indicates a feedback bound. A candidate that continues to advance
despite contrary measurements indicates a rejection bound. The framework in
@sec-architecture-20-ontology through @sec-feedback-verification-trust defines
how to represent and act on those distinctions.

@fig-data-movement-energy-scale should be read as an order-of-magnitude
comparison, not a current-node prediction to memorize. Local arithmetic and
memory movement occupy very different energy scales. In the cited 45\ nm data,
a 32-bit integer add costs a fraction of a picojoule, an on-chip SRAM access
costs two to three orders of magnitude more, and an off-chip DRAM access costs
roughly four orders of magnitude more than the add. Advanced nodes do not make
locality irrelevant. Moving data, driving wires, and supplying memory systems
remain substantial architectural costs. The 3\ nm-class lighthouse study would
therefore need a current power model before supporting a design decision.

```{python}
#| label: fig-data-movement-energy-scale
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Data movement can dominate arithmetic energy:** Rough Horowitz 45\ nm energy values show why architecture loops must represent locality, buffering, precision, scheduling, and memory hierarchy rather than counting arithmetic alone [@Horowitz2014Energy]. The values are transcribed order-of-magnitude anchors, including the source's 1.3--2.6\ nJ DRAM range, not current-node device estimates.
#| out-width: "92%"
#| fig-alt: "Log-scale plot comparing arithmetic and memory-access energy, showing a 32-bit integer add at 0.1 picojoules, SRAM accesses from 10 to 100 picojoules, and an off-chip DRAM access range from 1.3 to 2.6 nanojoules."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style, draw_range_rows, row_axis, top_log_axis

# Source receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter2-horowitz-energy.csv
rows = [
    {"display_label": "32b integer add", "display_note": "baseline arithmetic", "energy_low_pj": 0.1, "energy_high_pj": 0.1, "right_label": "1$\\times$", "color": COLORS["blue"]},
    {"display_label": "32b floating-point add", "display_note": "local arithmetic", "energy_low_pj": 0.9, "energy_high_pj": 0.9, "right_label": "9$\\times$", "color": COLORS["blue"]},
    {"display_label": "32b integer multiply", "display_note": "local arithmetic", "energy_low_pj": 3.0, "energy_high_pj": 3.0, "right_label": "30$\\times$", "color": COLORS["blue"]},
    {"display_label": "32b floating-point multiply", "display_note": "local arithmetic", "energy_low_pj": 4.0, "energy_high_pj": 4.0, "right_label": "40$\\times$", "color": COLORS["blue"]},
    {"display_label": "8 KB SRAM access", "display_note": "64b cache access", "energy_low_pj": 10, "energy_high_pj": 10, "right_label": "100$\\times$", "color": COLORS["green"]},
    {"display_label": "32 KB SRAM access", "display_note": "64b cache access", "energy_low_pj": 20, "energy_high_pj": 20, "right_label": "200$\\times$", "color": COLORS["green"]},
    {"display_label": "1 MB SRAM access", "display_note": "on-chip memory access", "energy_low_pj": 100, "energy_high_pj": 100, "right_label": "1000$\\times$", "color": COLORS["orange"]},
    {"display_label": "off-chip DRAM access", "display_note": "off-chip memory access", "energy_low_pj": 1300, "energy_high_pj": 2600, "right_label": "13k--26k$\\times$", "color": COLORS["red"]},
]

apply_style()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.9, 3.45))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.40, right=0.80, top=0.82, bottom=0.17)

row_axis(ax, len(rows))
top_log_axis(
    ax,
    xlim=(0.06, 4000),
    xticks=[0.1, 1, 10, 100, 1000],
    xticklabels=["0.1 pJ", "1 pJ", "10 pJ", "100 pJ", "1 nJ"],
    xlabel="rough energy per operation or access",
)
draw_range_rows(ax, rows, low_key="energy_low_pj", high_key="energy_high_pj", label_x=-0.82, right_x=1.04, label_fontsize=6.6, note_fontsize=5.4, right_fontsize=6.1, label_dy=-0.17, note_dy=0.23)
add_note_box(fig, "Rough 45 nm values from Horowitz; use for orders of magnitude.", xywh=(0.14, 0.035, 0.72, 0.075), fontsize=5.5)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

The practical conclusion is direct. A study that cannot estimate memory
movement should not support a power or efficiency decision, because poor
locality can overturn an apparent compute advantage.

## Engineering Cost Makes Feedback Scarce

Feedback becomes scarce when a study demands more engineering work than the
available people and tools can provide. @fig-scissors-gap compares the growing
burden of design choices, constraints, software paths, and physical feasibility
with the capacity to measure and review them. The first can grow without a
matching increase in the second.

![**The scissors gap is a feedback gap:** Design choices, constraints, and evidence demands can grow faster than a team can coordinate and verify them. The widening region marks that difference. @fig-verification-demand-scissors compares it with measured staffing demand.](images/F2-scissors-gap){#fig-scissors-gap width="92%" fig-alt="Scissors-style diagram where design choices, constraints, and evidence demands grow faster than manual coordination and verification capacity."}

Leading-edge chip-development costs show the scale of this constraint. Public
estimates vary and should not be treated as universal accounting rules. The
Semiconductor Industry Association reports that the cost of designing a
latest-node chip rose from about \$30M for a 65\ nm chip in 2006 to more than
\$540M for a 5\ nm chip in 2020, a greater than $18\times$ increase
[@SemiconductorIndustryAssociation2026ChipDesign]. A McKinsey analysis
gives a similar order of magnitude, estimating roughly \$175M for a 10\ nm
design, \$300M for a 7\ nm design, and \$540M for a 5\ nm design when validation,
IP qualification, and related development costs are included
[@BauerEtAl2020SemiconductorDesignManufacturing]. These estimates include more
than masks or wafers. They reflect architecture, design, validation,
verification, IP, tools, and engineering labor.

Verification staffing makes the people cost visible. Foster's summary of the
2022 Wilson Research Group functional-verification study reports that demand
for IC/ASIC verification engineers grew nearly three times as fast as demand
for design engineers from 2007 to 2022
(@fig-verification-demand-scissors). Mean peak staffing was roughly one
verification engineer per design engineer across most market segments,
processor projects reached ratios as high as five to one, and design engineers
spent 49 percent of their time on verification in 2022
[@Foster2022WilsonVerificationStudy]. Every invalid candidate that reaches
these stages consumes compute and scarce engineering time. Generated candidates
are cheap only until they require tools, verification, and expert review.

```{python}
#| label: fig-verification-demand-scissors
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Verification staffing grew faster than design staffing.** Mean peak demand for IC/ASIC verification engineers grew about 146 percent from 2007 to 2022 while demand for design engineers grew about 50 percent, a 6.2 versus 2.7 percent compound annual growth rate [@Foster2022WilsonVerificationStudy]. Filled markers have printed data labels in the published study figures; hollow 2022 markers are chart-read approximations constrained by the study's stated growth figures.
#| out-width: "78%"
#| fig-alt: "Two-line chart indexing engineer demand to 2007, with verification-engineer demand rising to about 2.5 times its 2007 level by 2022 while design-engineer demand reaches about 1.5 times, opening a widening gap between the lines."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style

apply_style()

# Mean peak engineers per IC/ASIC project, Wilson Research Group / Siemens EDA
# functional-verification studies (Foster's published summaries). 2007-2020
# values have printed data labels; 2022 values are chart-read, constrained by
# the study's stated callouts of +50% design and +146% verification demand
# growth since 2007 (2.7% vs 6.2% CAGR).
# Receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter2-wilson-verification-staffing.csv
years = [2007, 2012, 2016, 2020, 2022]
design = [7.8, 8.5, 10.5, 10.3, 11.7]
verif = [4.8, 8.4, 11.6, 11.4, 11.8]

design_idx = [v / design[0] for v in design]
verif_idx = [v / verif[0] for v in verif]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.65, 2.3))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.12, right=0.80, top=0.93, bottom=0.19)

ax.plot(years, verif_idx, color=COLORS["green"], linewidth=1.6, zorder=2)
ax.plot(years, design_idx, color=COLORS["blue"], linewidth=1.6, zorder=2)
ax.scatter(years[:-1], verif_idx[:-1], s=22, color=COLORS["green"], zorder=3)
ax.scatter(years[:-1], design_idx[:-1], s=20, marker="s", color=COLORS["blue"], zorder=3)
# 2022 values are chart-read approximations: draw them hollow
ax.scatter([years[-1]], [verif_idx[-1]], s=26, facecolors="white",
           edgecolors=COLORS["green"], linewidths=1.1, zorder=3)
ax.scatter([years[-1]], [design_idx[-1]], s=24, marker="s", facecolors="white",
           edgecolors=COLORS["blue"], linewidths=1.1, zorder=3)

ax.text(2022.6, verif_idx[-1], "verification\nengineers (+146%)", va="center",
        fontsize=6.0, color=COLORS["green"], clip_on=False)
ax.text(2022.6, design_idx[-1], "design\nengineers (+50%)", va="center",
        fontsize=6.0, color=COLORS["blue"], clip_on=False)

ax.set_xlim(2006, 2023)
ax.set_ylim(0.9, 2.7)
ax.set_xticks([2007, 2012, 2016, 2020, 2022])
ax.set_yticks([1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5])
ax.set_yticklabels(["1.0$\\times$", "1.5$\\times$", "2.0$\\times$", "2.5$\\times$"])
ax.set_ylabel("mean peak demand vs. 2007", fontsize=7)
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6.5, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.6)

for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

This staffing pressure matters because a defect that escapes early review
becomes harder and more expensive to correct after the design advances.

::: {.callout-field-note title="The bug five entries wide: Pentium FDIV"}
The 1994 Pentium FDIV flaw arose from five omitted entries in the lookup table
used by its division algorithm [@Edelman1997PentiumDivision]. Intel later
reported a \$475M pretax charge covering replacement and other associated costs
[@Intel1995PentiumCharge].

The episode shows how a missed early check can turn a small defect into a large
commitment cost.
:::

The Pentium charge reflects one escaped defect, but leading-edge development
raises the cost of commitment even when no public recall occurs. A 2023 report
of an IBS estimate places the cost of a large 2\ nm chip developed from scratch
without reusable IP at roughly \$725M
(@fig-leading-node-design-cost) [@Shilov2023IBSDesignCost]. The estimate is not a
universal project price. It shows that feedback and commitment near
leading-edge implementation consume engineering budgets, not only simulator
cycles.

```{python}
#| label: fig-leading-node-design-cost
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Selected leading-node design-cost estimates make the scissors concrete:** Public SIA and McKinsey estimates [@SemiconductorIndustryAssociation2026ChipDesign; @BauerEtAl2020SemiconductorDesignManufacturing] put chip design costs from tens of millions of dollars at 65 nm to more than $500M at 5 nm. A secondary report of an IBS estimate supplies the 2 nm point, roughly $725M for a large chip developed from scratch without reusable IP [@Shilov2023IBSDesignCost]. Accounting boundaries, dates, and project assumptions vary, so the connected marks are scale anchors rather than a controlled time series or universal project prices.
#| out-width: "86%"
#| fig-alt: "Connected plot of selected public chip design-cost estimates: about 30 million dollars at 65 nanometers, 175 million at 10 nanometers, 300 million at 7 nanometers, 540 million at 5 nanometers, and a secondary estimate of 725 million at 2 nanometers."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style

# Source receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter2-leading-node-design-cost.csv
rows = [
    {"node_label": "65 nm", "cost_musd": 30, "source": "SIA", "right_label": "~$30M"},
    {"node_label": "10 nm", "cost_musd": 175, "source": "McKinsey", "right_label": "~$175M"},
    {"node_label": "7 nm", "cost_musd": 300, "source": "McKinsey", "right_label": "~$300M"},
    {"node_label": "5 nm", "cost_musd": 540, "source": "SIA / McKinsey", "right_label": ">$540M"},
    {"node_label": "2 nm", "cost_musd": 725, "source": "IBS (secondary)", "right_label": "~$725M"},
]

apply_style()
labels = [row["node_label"] for row in rows]
costs = [row["cost_musd"] for row in rows]
sources = [row["source"] for row in rows]
right_labels = [row["right_label"] for row in rows]
x_values = list(range(len(rows)))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.65, 2.25))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.14, right=0.95, top=0.88, bottom=0.33)

ax.plot(x_values, costs, color=COLORS["blue"], linewidth=1.7, marker="s", markersize=4.5, markerfacecolor=COLORS["note_fill"], markeredgewidth=1.0)
ax.fill_between(x_values, costs, [0] * len(costs), color=COLORS["design_cost_fill"], alpha=0.75, zorder=0)
ax.set_xlim(-0.35, len(rows) - 0.65)
ax.set_ylim(0, 800)
ax.set_xticks(x_values)
ax.set_xticklabels(labels, fontsize=6.8)
ax.set_yticks([0, 100, 300, 500, 700])
ax.set_yticklabels(["$0", "$100M", "$300M", "$500M", "$700M"], fontsize=6.5)
ax.set_ylabel("public design-cost estimate", fontsize=7)
ax.tick_params(axis="both", length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.6)

for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])

for x, cost, source, right_label in zip(x_values, costs, sources, right_labels):
    ax.text(x, cost + 24, right_label, ha="center", va="bottom", fontsize=6.6, fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["ink"])
    ax.text(x, -83, source, ha="center", va="top", fontsize=5.7, color=COLORS["muted"], clip_on=False)

add_note_box(fig, "Directional estimates: accounting boundaries vary by chip, node, IP reuse, and organization.", xywh=(0.16, 0.04, 0.72, 0.12), fontsize=5.8)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

These costs explain why feedback can be scarce even when candidates are cheap
to generate. If the relevant tools, verification effort, or review time cannot
keep up, the loop is feedback-bound before added proposals enter it.

The AlphaChip debate separates the speed of producing a placement from the
strength of the comparison used to evaluate it.

::: {.callout-war-story title="Faster placement did not settle the comparison"}
**The claim.** The 2021 AlphaChip paper reported that a reinforcement-learning
method could produce macro placements in hours and achieve results comparable
to or better than expert placements [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement].

**The gap.** A later independent assessment found that a strong
simulated-annealing baseline remained competitive or better on most of its
public test cases. The original authors argued that the reassessment did not
reproduce the published method and setting
[@ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL; @GoldieEtAl2024ThatChipHasSailed].

**The lesson.** Generating more placements could not resolve the disagreement.
The public comparisons did not share all of the cases, budgets,
implementations, and physical-design checks needed to reach one conclusion.
AlphaChip greatly reduced the time required to produce a candidate, but that
achievement did not settle the disputed comparison.
:::

The public dispute does not establish that learned placement is ineffective,
and it does not measure proposal-arrival rates or reviewer capacity. It shows
that faster generation and a settled comparison are different achievements. In
the executed study in @sec-running-the-loop, the AI and conventional arms
receive equal proposal and SCALE-Sim budgets. They reach the same architectural
result before a separate test of the proposed explanation. More proposals would
not settle whether AI helped or whether that explanation was correct. When
feedback is already scarce, admitting more proposals can move the pressure to
rejection.

## Rejection Cannot Keep Up With Proposals {#sec-feedback-and-verification-become-the-bottleneck}

A feedback problem becomes a rejection problem when comparable proposals arrive
faster than the available checks and reviewers can settle them. The unresolved
queue then grows. Confirming this diagnosis requires measured arrival and review
rates, changes in queue length, and reasons for rejection. No single throughput
limit applies to every study. Depending on the cause, a team may admit fewer
proposals, increase the capacity of an independent check, or reduce the cost of
a reliable review.

Verification costs limit how quickly teams can evaluate and reject proposals.
Across the same study series, first-silicon success fell from roughly one third
of IC/ASIC projects to 14 percent in 2024
(@fig-first-silicon-trend). The series reports outcomes rather than causes. A
failed first spin shows that earlier checks left some important risk unresolved,
but the survey cannot identify whether the failure arose in design,
verification, integration, physical implementation, manufacturing, scheduling,
or organization. A team must measure evaluation and review capacity within its
own study rather than infer saturation from this trend.

Architecture measurements differ in cost and in the conclusions they can
support. A spreadsheet model is cheap but limited. Simulation may be more
informative, but it can be slow or biased. Synthesis and physical design expose
implementation effects, yet their results depend on settings and constraints.
Silicon and deployment telemetry arrive only after major commitments.
@sec-feedback-verification-trust explains how these observations support
claims. Here the immediate question is whether the review process can keep up
with the proposals it admits.

```{python}
#| label: fig-first-silicon-trend
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **First-silicon success keeps falling:** The share of surveyed IC/ASIC projects achieving first-silicon success fell from roughly one third, near the two-decade norm, to 14 percent in 2024, the lowest level the study series has recorded [@Foster2022WilsonVerificationStudy; @Foster2025WilsonVerification2024]. Filled markers are values the reports state directly; hollow markers are chart-read approximations from published study figures. A failed first spin shows that earlier checks did not settle every relevant risk; the survey does not identify the causal bottleneck.
#| out-width: "78%"
#| fig-alt: "Line chart of first-silicon success rates for IC/ASIC projects from 2012 to 2024, falling from about a third of projects to 14 percent in 2024, below a dashed 30 percent two-decade reference line."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style

apply_style()

# First-silicon success rate (% of IC/ASIC projects), Wilson Research Group /
# Siemens EDA functional-verification studies (Foster's published summaries).
# 2020, 2022, and 2024 values are stated in the reports; 2012-2018 values are
# chart-read approximations from unlabeled study figures; ~30% is the stated
# two-decade historical norm.
# Receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter2-wilson-first-silicon-success.csv
chart_read = [(2012, 33.5), (2014, 31), (2016, 33), (2018, 26)]
stated = [(2020, 32), (2022, 24), (2024, 14)]
series = sorted(chart_read + stated)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.65, 2.3))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.13, right=0.95, top=0.93, bottom=0.19)

ax.axhline(30, color=COLORS["muted"], linewidth=0.9, linestyle=(0, (4, 3)), zorder=1)
ax.text(2024.8, 31.0, "~30% two-decade historical norm", fontsize=5.8,
        color=COLORS["muted"], ha="right", va="bottom")

ax.plot([p[0] for p in series], [p[1] for p in series], color=COLORS["red"],
        linewidth=1.6, zorder=2)
ax.scatter([p[0] for p in stated], [p[1] for p in stated], s=24,
           color=COLORS["red"], zorder=3)
ax.scatter([p[0] for p in chart_read], [p[1] for p in chart_read], s=24,
           facecolors="white", edgecolors=COLORS["red"], linewidths=1.1, zorder=3)
ax.annotate("14%", (2024, 14), textcoords="offset points", xytext=(-2, -11),
            fontsize=6.4, fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["red"])

ax.set_xlim(2011, 2025)
ax.set_ylim(0, 42)
ax.set_xticks([2012, 2016, 2020, 2024])
ax.set_yticks([0, 10, 20, 30, 40])
ax.set_yticklabels(["0%", "10%", "20%", "30%", "40%"])
ax.set_ylabel("first-silicon success", fontsize=7)
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6.5, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.6)

for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

Because the first-silicon survey does not identify the cause of failure, a team
must diagnose its own study before increasing candidate generation.

::: {#pri-treat-feedback .callout-design-principle title="Diagnose the study before adding AI"}
First identify which part of the work is slowing the study, whether that is
representing the problem, producing valid candidates, obtaining useful
feedback, rejecting weak results, or making a decision. Use AI only when it
relieves that constraint without increasing verification, review, or decision
pressure elsewhere.
:::

## Architecture Violates Generic AI Assumptions

A conventional machine-learning workflow provides a useful comparison.
Students often learn a pipeline from data collection and preprocessing through
training, validation, deployment, and monitoring. That pipeline remains useful,
but it does not capture all of an architecture study
(@fig-ai-workflow-mismatch). Architects must also know which design state was
represented, which actions were legal, what each measurement cost, how the
workload changed, why candidates were rejected, and who can act on the final
claim. @tbl-ai-assumptions-architecture-violations compares common assumptions
in a generic pipeline with the corresponding architecture constraint and the
response it requires.

![**An architecture study needs more information than a generic ML workflow.** In addition to data, preprocessing, training, validation, and deployment, the study must preserve the design state, legal choices, feedback cost, rejected candidates, workload changes, architectural claim, and person responsible for the decision.](images/F2c-ai-workflow-mismatch){#fig-ai-workflow-mismatch width="100%" fig-alt="Comparison diagram showing an architecture study with design state, legal choices, costly feedback, rejected candidates, workload changes, claim review, and a named decision role alongside a generic machine-learning pipeline."}

Architecture rarely has the conditions assumed by an introductory
supervised-learning pipeline or a benchmarked AI task. Available datasets may
be proprietary, incomplete, stale, or omit the failed and rejected candidates
that reveal the boundaries of the design space.
Feedback can range from a quick proxy to simulation, synthesis, a
physical-design report, expert review, emulation, or silicon measurement. Each
has different latency, cost, fidelity, and authority. Many generated
configurations are also illegal, unsupported by tools, unverifiable, or
incompatible with software and physical constraints.

Community benchmarks demonstrate the difference. SPEC CPU and MLPerf provide
more than inputs. They specify run rules, reporting conventions, versioned
tasks, and a basis for comparing claims [@SPEC2017CPU;
@MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf]. An AI-assisted architecture study needs similar
clarity about the state, actions, measurements, review outcomes, and decisions
behind a reported result.

The assumptions of a generic AI workflow fail at different points in different
architecture projects. The chapters from
@sec-data-representations-world-models through
@sec-feedback-verification-trust develop the representations, environments,
methods, and review practices needed to address these problems.

| **Common AI assumption** | **Architecture violation** | **Loop implication** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Abundant labeled data | Many labels are proprietary, expensive, stale, or not recorded. | Check whether the available state covers the decision. |
| Cheap feedback | Simulation, synthesis, EDA, emulation, and review can be slow or scarce. | Check whether measurement capacity matches decision demand. |
| Stable distribution | Workloads, software stacks, compilers, models, and deployment policies drift. | Revisit decisions when their workload assumptions change. |
| Valid actions are easy to define | Many generated configurations are invalid, unsafe, unverifiable, or unsupported by tools. | Separate legal choices from invalid or unsupported moves. |
| Reward is clear | Efficiency mixes performance, energy, area, cost, reliability, sustainability, verification burden, and risk. | State which tradeoffs the decision is allowed to make. |
| Proxy metrics are enough | Proxy wins can vanish under stronger checks or different workloads. | Record which conclusions a proxy cannot settle. |
| Failures are just bad samples | Failed runs, rejected candidates, and invalid states describe the design space. | Preserve why alternatives failed or were rejected. |

: **Architecture studies must account for non-ideal machine-learning conditions:** The study must handle proprietary or incomplete data, expensive feedback, invalid actions, changing workloads, multiobjective efficiency, proxy failures, and rejected candidates. {#tbl-ai-assumptions-architecture-violations tbl-colwidths="[28,31,30]"}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

AI remains useful when its contribution matches the problem. It can summarize
tool outputs, propose candidates, search a design space, predict costs, construct
tests, critique assumptions, retrieve prior designs, or coordinate subtasks.
The benefit depends on the diagnosed bound. Missing workload state requires
fresh workload characterization. If the needed design lies outside the allowed
choices, the architect must revise the design space. A growing review queue
requires tighter admission, cheaper checks, or more reviewer capacity.
@sec-architecture-20-ontology defines the information needed to make this
distinction for a concrete claim.

## Open Research Questions

Research on AI-assisted architecture should begin with a practical question.
When is assistance warranted, where should it enter the study, and which signs
of pressure should trigger stronger evidence before expensive validation or a
human decision?

1. **What measurements reveal a strained architecture loop?**
A loop becomes strained when candidate volume, feedback cost, validation
burden, software drift, or human review latency grows faster than the team's
ability to reject and revise. Measurements such as invalid-action rates,
feedback latency and cost, verification staffing, high-fidelity sample budgets,
and decision turnaround should be tested for their ability to predict wasted
high-fidelity runs, missed invalid candidates, delayed decisions, and stale
evidence. Together they should explain the scissors gap in @fig-scissors-gap
and the cost pressure in @fig-leading-node-design-cost better than candidate
count alone.

2. **Where should AI enter specialized and chiplet design loops?**
Specialization and chiplets connect workload selection, software paths,
microarchitecture, integration, packaging, validation, and physical
feasibility. Candidate AI entry points should be compared under the same
validation budget. The comparison should report invalid candidates avoided,
software obligations missed, high-fidelity checks saved, and any loss in
tradeoff quality. @tbl-design-loop-scale-anchors and
@tbl-loop-bottleneck-diagnostic provide concrete test cases for this work.

3. **Which physical signals should triage expensive validation?**
Data movement, timing margin, congestion, power density, thermal behavior, and
closure risk can all overturn an early architectural result. Research should
test which early signals predict later synthesis, floorplanning, placement,
routing, static timing analysis, design-rule checking,
layout-versus-schematic checking, electromigration and voltage drop (EM/IR),
and power outcomes. The evaluation should report false accepts, false rejects,
inconclusive runs, and engineering effort saved. The purpose is to decide
earlier whether to reject a candidate, revise it, or gather stronger evidence,
not to replace signoff.

4. **When does workload drift expire architecture evidence?**
The software and workload pressure described in
@sec-software-changes-faster-than-silicon can make architecture evidence stale
before deployment. One line of research could estimate how long a claim remains
valid from workload versions, input-distribution summaries, compiler and
runtime assumptions, field telemetry, and re-evaluation triggers. Historical
workload changes or fleet signals could then test when the claim should be
maintained, weakened, or withdrawn. Such a method would make workload
characterization part of the evidence for a decision.

## Conclusion

This chapter asked which part of an architecture loop cannot keep up before AI
is added. Specialization, heterogeneous integration, data movement, software
velocity, physical constraints, and validation burden can strain the state a
study represents, the choices it permits, the measurements it can afford, the
candidates it can settle, or the decisions it can justify.

The five diagnoses keep different problems separate. Missing workload state
calls for better representation, while an inadequate choice set requires a
change to the design space. Scarce measurements call for a different response
than a saturated review queue or evidence that cannot support a clear decision.

AI assistance is useful when it relieves the diagnosed bound without moving the
problem elsewhere. Another architect should be able to inspect the study's
state, failures, evidence, rejected candidates, and decision authority and judge
whether the assistance helped. Adding automation should reduce unresolved work
and lead to better-supported findings and decisions.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Diagnose whether representation, action, feedback,
  rejection, or commitment cannot keep up before deciding where AI belongs.
- **Reader test:** Can you identify the first bound in a strained architecture
  loop and explain why relieving a different one would shift the pressure?
- **Up next:** @sec-architecture-20-ontology distinguishes a study's scope,
  artifacts, architectural claim,
  execution loop, evidence, and decision boundary.
:::
